Totient-sigma congruence with Hensel/2-adic/order-coupled square filter

Let $n=11^3\cdot 19\cdot 29$. Let $\varphi(n)$ denote Euler's totient function, let $\sigma(n)$ denote the sum of positive divisors of $n$, and for a prime $\ell$ let $v_\ell(m)$ denote the exponent of $\ell$ in the prime factorization of the nonzero integer $m$. For $\gcd(y,19)=1$, let $\operatorname{ord}_{19}(y)$ denote the multiplicative order of $y$ modulo $19$.

Consider the congruence
$$
\varphi(n)\,x \equiv 11^2\cdot 19\cdot 29\cdot \sigma(n)\pmod{n}.
$$
Among all integers $x$ satisfying this congruence such that
(1) $v_{19}(x)=v_{29}(x)=1$,
(2) $x/(19\cdot 29)$ is a perfect square, so $x/(19\cdot 29)=y^2$ for some integer $y>0$,
(3) $y^2\equiv 3\pmod{11^2}$,
(4) $y^2\equiv 25\pmod{32}$,
(5) $\operatorname{ord}_{19}(y)=9$,
let $x_0$ be the least positive such $x$.

Determine the unique integer $N\in\{0,1,\dots,998\}$ such that $N\equiv x_0\pmod{999}$.

Solution.
Lemma 1 (a totient gcd bottleneck for $p^kqr$).
Let $p,q,r$ be distinct odd primes with $p<q<r$ and let $k\ge 2$. Put $n=p^kqr$ and assume
(i) $p\nmid (q-1)(r-1)$ and (ii) $q\nmid (r-1)$.
Then $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1}$.

Proof.
By multiplicativity,
$\varphi(n)=\varphi(p^k)\varphi(q)\varphi(r)=p^{k-1}(p-1)(q-1)(r-1)$.
Because $p\nmid (q-1)(r-1)$ and $p\nmid (p-1)$, we have $v_p(\varphi(n))=k-1$ while $v_p(n)=k$, so the $p$-part of $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)$ is $p^{k-1}$.
If $q\mid \varphi(n)$, then $q$ divides $p^{k-1}(p-1)(q-1)(r-1)$, hence $q\mid (r-1)$ because $q\nmid p^{k-1}$, $q\nmid(p-1)$ (since $0<p-1<q$), and $q\nmid(q-1)$. This contradicts (ii). Hence $q\nmid \varphi(n)$.
Similarly $r\nmid\varphi(n)$ because $r>q>p$ implies $r\nmid(p-1)$ and $r\nmid(q-1)$, and also $r\nmid(r-1)$.
Therefore $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1}$.  □

Lemma 2 (Hensel lifting for square roots modulo $p^2$, simple-root case).
Let $p$ be an odd prime and let $a$ be an integer. Suppose $y_0^2\equiv a\pmod p$ and $p\nmid 2y_0$ (equivalently $y_0\not\equiv 0\pmod p$). Then there exists a unique residue class $y\pmod{p^2}$ with $y\equiv y_0\pmod p$ and $y^2\equiv a\pmod{p^2}$.
Writing $y=y_0+pt$, this unique lift is determined by the linear congruence
$$
2y_0\,t \equiv \frac{a-y_0^2}{p}\pmod p.
$$

Proof.
Compute $(y_0+pt)^2=y_0^2+2py_0t+p^2t^2\equiv y_0^2+2py_0t\pmod{p^2}$.
Thus $y^2\equiv a\pmod{p^2}$ is equivalent to $2y_0t\equiv (a-y_0^2)/p\pmod p$, and since $p\nmid 2y_0$, the coefficient $2y_0$ is invertible mod $p$, giving a unique $t\pmod p$.  □

Lemma 3 (odd squares modulo $32$).
For odd $y$, $y^2\equiv 1,9,17,$ or $25\pmod{32}$. In particular,
$y^2\equiv 25\pmod{32}$ if and only if $y\equiv 5,11,21,$ or $27\pmod{32}$.

Proof.
Every odd residue modulo $32$ is congruent to one of $1,3,5,\dots,31$. Squaring shows the only possible odd square residues mod $32$ are $1,9,17,25$, and a direct check gives exactly $y\equiv 5,11,21,27\pmod{32}$ for the residue $25$.  □

Lemma 4 (elements of order $9$ modulo $19$).
The group $(\mathbb{Z}/19\mathbb{Z})^\times$ is cyclic of order $18$. Its elements of order exactly $9$ are
$$
\{4,5,6,9,16,17\}\pmod{19}.
$$

Proof.
Since $19$ is prime, $(\mathbb{Z}/19\mathbb{Z})^\times$ is cyclic of order $18$.
Compute $2^9=512\equiv 18\equiv -1\pmod{19}$, so $2^{18}\equiv 1\pmod{19}$ and $2^9\not\equiv 1\pmod{19}$; hence $\operatorname{ord}_{19}(2)=18$.
Then $4=2^2$ has order $18/\gcd(18,2)=9$, so the unique subgroup of order $9$ is $\langle 4\rangle$.
The elements of order $9$ in this subgroup are $4^m$ with $\gcd(m,9)=1$, i.e. $m\in\{1,2,4,5,7,8\}$.
Computing $4^m\pmod{19}$ gives $4,16,9,17,6,5$ respectively, as claimed.  □

Step 1 (multiplicativity of $\varphi$ and $\sigma$).
Because $n=11^3\cdot 19\cdot 29$ is a product of coprime prime powers,
$\varphi(n)=\varphi(11^3)\varphi(19)\varphi(29)$ and $\sigma(n)=\sigma(11^3)\sigma(19)\sigma(29)$.

Step 2 (evaluate the prime-power pieces).
We have $\varphi(11^3)=11^2(11-1)=1210$, $\varphi(19)=18$, $\varphi(29)=28$, hence
$\varphi(n)=1210\cdot 18\cdot 28=609840$.
Also $\sigma(11^3)=(11^4-1)/(11-1)=(14641-1)/10=1464$, $\sigma(19)=1+19=20$, $\sigma(29)=1+29=30$, hence
$\sigma(n)=1464\cdot 20\cdot 30=878400$.

Step 3 (compute $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)$ using Lemma 1).
Apply Lemma 1 with $(p,k,q,r)=(11,3,19,29)$.
Check the guards:
$11\nmid (19-1)(29-1)=18\cdot 28$ and $19\nmid (29-1)=28$.
Therefore $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)=11^{3-1}=11^2=121$.

Step 4 (reduce the linear congruence).
A congruence $Ax\equiv B\pmod n$ is solvable iff $\gcd(A,n)\mid B$, and then it reduces by dividing through by $d=\gcd(A,n)$.
Here $A=\varphi(n)$ and $B=11^2\cdot 19\cdot 29\cdot \sigma(n)$, so $121\mid B$ is immediate.
Divide the congruence and modulus by $121$ to get
$$
\frac{\varphi(n)}{121}\,x \equiv 19\cdot 29\cdot \sigma(n)\pmod{\frac{n}{121}}.
$$
Compute $\varphi(n)/121=609840/121=5040$ and $n/121=11\cdot 19\cdot 29=6061$, so
$$
5040\,x \equiv 551\cdot 878400\pmod{6061}.
$$

Step 5 (show the reduced coefficient is invertible mod $6061$).
We check invertibility modulo each prime factor of $6061$:
$5040\equiv 2\pmod{11}$, $5040\equiv 5\pmod{19}$, and $5040\equiv 23\pmod{29}$, so none of $11,19,29$ divides $5040$.
Thus $\gcd(5040,6061)=1$, and the reduced congruence has a unique solution modulo $6061$.

Step 6 (force divisibility by $19$ and $29$).
Reduce the congruence from Step 4 modulo $19$ and $29$.
Because the right-hand side contains the factor $551=19\cdot 29$, it is $0$ modulo both $19$ and $29$.
Since $5040$ is invertible modulo $19$ and $29$, we can cancel and obtain
$x\equiv 0\pmod{19}$ and $x\equiv 0\pmod{29}$.
Hence $551\mid x$, so write $x=551t$.

Step 7 (determine $t$ modulo $11$).
Reduce Step 4 modulo $11$. Since $551\equiv 1\pmod{11}$, substituting $x=551t$ gives
$5040t\equiv 878400\pmod{11}$.
Now $5040\equiv 2\pmod{11}$ and $878400\equiv 6\pmod{11}$, so $2t\equiv 6\pmod{11}$ and thus $t\equiv 3\pmod{11}$.
Write $t=3+11s$. Then every solution of the original congruence has the form
$$
x=551(3+11s)=1653+6061s,\qquad s\in\mathbb{Z}.
$$

Step 8 (translate the square and valuation filters).
The condition $x/(19\cdot 29)$ is a perfect square means $x/551$ is a square.
From Step 7, $x/551=3+11s$, so there is an integer $y>0$ with
$$
3+11s=y^2,\qquad x=551y^2.
$$
Because $y^2\ge 1$, we must have $3+11s>0$, hence $s\ge 0$.
The conditions $v_{19}(x)=v_{29}(x)=1$ become $\gcd(y,19)=\gcd(y,29)=1$ (since $x=19\cdot 29\cdot y^2$).

Step 9 (use the extra $11^2$-constraint to force a second coupling).
We are given $y^2\equiv 3\pmod{11^2}$, i.e. $y^2\equiv 3\pmod{121}$.
But $y^2-3=11s$, so $121\mid 11s$, hence $11\mid s$.
Write $s=11t$ with $t\ge 0$, so
$$
y^2=3+121t,\qquad x=551y^2.
$$
Equivalently, $y$ must satisfy $y^2\equiv 3\pmod{121}$.

Step 10 (solve $y^2\equiv 3\pmod{121}$ via Hensel lifting).
First solve modulo $11$: $5^2=25\equiv 3\pmod{11}$, so $y\equiv \pm 5\pmod{11}$.
Lift $y_0=5$ to modulo $121$ using Lemma 2. Let $y=5+11u$; then
$y^2\equiv 25+110u\pmod{121}$.
We need $25+110u\equiv 3\pmod{121}$, i.e. $110u\equiv -22\pmod{121}$.
Dividing by $11$ gives $10u\equiv -2\equiv 9\pmod{11}$, hence $u\equiv 2\pmod{11}$ and so $y\equiv 27\pmod{121}$.
The other root is its negative: $y\equiv -27\equiv 94\pmod{121}$.
Thus the solutions are exactly
$$
y\equiv 27\pmod{121}\quad\text{or}\quad y\equiv 94\pmod{121}.
$$

Step 11 (impose the $2$-adic square class).
We are also given $y^2\equiv 25\pmod{32}$. By Lemma 3, this is equivalent to
$$
y\equiv 5,11,21,\text{ or }27\pmod{32}.
$$

Step 12 (impose the order condition modulo $19$).
We are given $\operatorname{ord}_{19}(y)=9$, so in particular $\gcd(y,19)=1$.
By Lemma 4, this is equivalent to
$$
y\equiv 4,5,6,9,16,\text{ or }17\pmod{19}.
$$

Step 13 (find the least positive $y$ satisfying Steps 10-12, and also $29\nmid y$).
We treat the two Hensel classes from Step 10 separately.

Case A: $y\equiv 27\pmod{121}$. Write $y=27+121a$ with $a\ge 0$.
Modulo $32$, since $121\equiv 25\pmod{32}$, we have $y\equiv 27+25a\pmod{32}$.
Requiring $y\equiv 5,11,21,$ or $27\pmod{32}$ is equivalent to
$a\equiv 0,10,16,$ or $26\pmod{32}$ (since $25\cdot 9\equiv 1\pmod{32}$, so $25$ is invertible modulo $32$).
Modulo $19$, since $27\equiv 8\pmod{19}$ and $121\equiv 7\pmod{19}$, we have $y\equiv 8+7a\pmod{19}$.
Requiring $y\equiv 4,5,6,9,16,$ or $17\pmod{19}$ is equivalent to
$a\equiv 4,5,11,12,13,$ or $16\pmod{19}$ (since $7\cdot 11\equiv 1\pmod{19}$, so $7$ is invertible modulo $19$).
To find the least $a\ge 0$ satisfying both modular restrictions, note that the four residue classes modulo $32$ start at $a=0,10,16,26$.
The candidates $a=0$ and $a=10$ are $<16$ but fail the mod-$19$ restriction, and any other integer in those same classes is at least $32$ larger (hence $\ge 32>16$).
The next class start is $a=16$, which satisfies the mod-$19$ restriction, so it is the least possible.
Thus the least possible $a$ in Case A is $a=16$, giving $y=27+121\cdot 16=1963$.
Also $1963\not\equiv 0\pmod{29}$, so the valuation condition at $29$ is satisfied.

Case B: $y\equiv 94\pmod{121}$. Write $y=94+121a$ with $a\ge 0$.
Modulo $32$, $94\equiv 30\pmod{32}$ and $121\equiv 25\pmod{32}$, so $y\equiv 30+25a\pmod{32}$.
Requiring $y\equiv 5,11,21,$ or $27\pmod{32}$ is equivalent to
$a\equiv 5,15,21,$ or $31\pmod{32}$ (again using $25\cdot 9\equiv 1\pmod{32}$).
Modulo $19$, $94\equiv -1\pmod{19}$ and $121\equiv 7\pmod{19}$, so $y\equiv -1+7a\pmod{19}$.
Requiring $y\equiv 4,5,6,9,16,$ or $17\pmod{19}$ is equivalent to
$a\equiv 1,8,9,15,16,$ or $17\pmod{19}$ (again using $7\cdot 11\equiv 1\pmod{19}$).
To find the least $a\ge 0$ satisfying both modular restrictions, note that the smallest candidate from the mod-$32$ list is $a=5$, but it fails the mod-$19$ restriction, and any other integer with $a\equiv 5\pmod{32}$ is at least $32$ larger (hence $\ge 37>15$).
The next mod-$32$ candidate is $a=15$, which satisfies the mod-$19$ restriction, so it is the least possible.
Therefore the least possible $a$ in Case B is $a=15$, giving $y=94+121\cdot 15=1909$.
Also $1909\not\equiv 0\pmod{29}$, so the valuation condition at $29$ is satisfied.

Comparing the least candidates, $1909<1963$, so the least positive integer $y$ satisfying all constraints is
$$
y_0=1909.
$$
Therefore
$$
x_0 = 551y_0^2 = 551\cdot 1909^2.
$$

Step 14 (compute $N=x_0\bmod 999$).
Reduce modulo $999$:
$1909\equiv 910\pmod{999}$, hence $1909^2\equiv 910^2=828100\equiv 928\pmod{999}$.
Then
$x_0\equiv 551\cdot 928\equiv 839\pmod{999}$.
Thus the unique $N\in\{0,1,\dots,998\}$ with $N\equiv x_0\pmod{999}$ is $N=839$.

Answer: $839$.

Uniqueness Test
1) By Lemma 1 and Step 3, $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)=121$, so the original congruence reduces to a congruence modulo $n/121=6061$.
Step 5 shows the reduced coefficient $5040$ is invertible modulo $6061$, so the reduced congruence has exactly one solution class modulo $6061$.
Steps 6-7 identify that class explicitly as $x=1653+6061s$.

2) The square filter forces $x=551y^2$ with $y>0$, and then $x$ is strictly increasing in $y$.
The condition $y^2\equiv 3\pmod{121}$ forces $y$ into exactly two residue classes modulo $121$ (Step 10).
The conditions $y^2\equiv 25\pmod{32}$ and $\operatorname{ord}_{19}(y)=9$ restrict $y$ to finite subsets modulo $32$ and $19$ (Steps 11-12).

3) In each Hensel class modulo $121$, writing $y=y_\ast+121a$ turns the constraints modulo $32$ and $19$ into explicit congruence restrictions on $a$.
Because $\gcd(32,19)=1$, these restrictions leave a discrete arithmetic progression of admissible $a$ values in each case; checking the finitely many smallest candidates in each case determines the least admissible $a$ uniquely (Step 13).
This yields a unique least positive admissible $y_0=1909$, and hence a unique least positive admissible $x_0=551y_0^2$.

4) Finally, reduction modulo $999$ with the normalization $0\le N\le 998$ is unique, so the output $N=839$ is uniquely determined.

839
